policy_statement: Maximize opportunities for citywide, non-exclusive access to linear and linked open space systems with convenient non-motorized connections.
mechanism_description: Maximizing linked open space systems with convenient non-motorized connections supports more walking and biking, shifting trips away from motor vehicles and reducing transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport infrastructure connectivity expansion → mode shift/↓ VMT → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city expands and improves connected, convenient non-motorized corridors (sidewalk networks, protected bike lanes, multi-use paths/greenways, safe crossings) so more trips can feasibly be made by walking/biking instead of driving.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced vehicle kilometers/miles traveled and/or increased occupancy/shorter car trips lowers gasoline/diesel combustion in the vehicle fleet.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower tailpipe CO₂ (and some upstream fuel-cycle emissions) from the transport sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced on-road fuel combustion from mode shift (and reduced vehicle travel) enabled by better walking/biking connectivity, leading to lower transport GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Non-motorized connectivity improvements induce modal shift and reduce VMT, cutting fossil-fuel combustion emissions from transport.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While such corridors may be “open space” and can yield minor nature/heat or stormwater co-benefits, the mechanism described is explicitly about enabling walking/biking as substitutes for motorized trips; the largest climate effect is emissions avoidance (mitigation), not adaptation, resource efficiency as a primary frame, or ecosystem carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented purely as recreational open space with negligible trip substitution, mitigation impact could be small; however, policies sharing this mechanism are defined by the connectivity→mode shift→emissions reduction chain, so mitigation remains the correct primary category.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that expands connected non-motorized networks with the intent/effect of shifting travel away from motor vehicles has the same dominant emissions-reduction pathway.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Secondary labels (e.g., health co-benefits, air quality, urban greening/NBS elements, equity, safety) require policy-specific evidence and are deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may emphasize recreation/amenity or urban greening; if mode shift/VMT reduction is not material, mitigation benefits may be limited, but the canonical mechanism as defined presumes connectivity-driven mode shift, keeping Mitigation as primary.
key_indicators: "Strategy"; "maximize opportunities"; "citywide...access"; "linear and linked open space systems"; "convenient non-motorized connection"
co_benefits: Improved public health (active travel), recreation access, traffic safety, local air-quality improvements, equity of access to open space
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via infrastructure investment (trails/greenways, bike/ped connections), but the text as written is an unquantified strategy statement rather than a specific capital commitment or regulatory requirement.
